content.text: Data preprocessing agents automate the most time-consuming phase of the machine learning workflow: transforming raw, messy data into a clean, feature-rich dataset ready for model training. Data preprocessing typically consumes 60-80% of an ML project's time — not because the operations are intellectually demanding, but because the problem space is large (missing values, encoding inconsistencies, distribution outliers, schema drift, duplicate records, label noise) and the decisions are contextual (the right imputation strategy for 'age' differs from the right strategy for 'transaction amount'). A preprocessing agent profiles the raw data, detects quality issues, reasons about appropriate transformations given the task context, executes the cleaning and feature engineering pipeline, and validates the output — converting hours of manual pandas scripting into a documented, reproducible pipeline.

attrs.alt: Data preprocessing agent pipeline: raw data sources feed an orchestrator that dispatches to Cleaning, Feature Engineering, Validation, and Quality Reporter agents, producing a versioned, audited processed dataset

content.text: A data preprocessing agent is an AI system that automates the transformation of raw data into training-ready datasets by orchestrating data profiling, quality issue detection, cleaning strategy selection, feature engineering, output validation, and dataset documentation. The agent operates on tabular, text, time-series, or multimodal data and adapts its transformation decisions to the task context (classification vs. regression, downstream model architecture, domain-specific constraints).

The agent does not apply transformations blindly — it reasons about the data's characteristics and the downstream task to select appropriate strategies. For missing values, it may choose mean imputation for continuous variables in a regression task, mode imputation for categorical variables, or forward-fill for time series — and logs the rationale for each decision. The resulting pipeline is not just a cleaned dataset but a reproducible, documented transformation history.

Data quality is the most underrated determinant of model quality. A state-of-the-art model trained on poorly preprocessed data will underperform a simpler model trained on well-prepared data. The challenge is not that cleaning is difficult — it is that the problem space is large and context-dependent, requiring decisions that are subtle enough to benefit from reasoning but repetitive enough to benefit from automation.

Without systematic preprocessing, teams repeatedly solve the same problems (handling missing values, encoding high-cardinality categoricals, normalizing skewed distributions) ad-hoc, producing pipelines that are hard to reproduce, poorly documented, and inconsistent across experiments. A preprocessing agent creates a systematic, queryable record of every transformation decision — enabling reproducibility, auditability, and continuous improvement.

Systematic coverage: the agent profiles every column and applies appropriate transformations based on data type, task context, and detected patterns — eliminating the case where a problematic column is missed under time pressure.
Reproducible pipelines: every transformation is logged with the decision rationale, enabling exact reproduction of the preprocessing steps for any training run.

Adaptive feature engineering: the agent can propose domain-appropriate features (time-based features for time series, interaction terms for tabular data, text embeddings for NLP) rather than requiring the engineer to enumerate them manually.

Data quality documentation: the agent generates a data card — a structured summary of the dataset's provenance, transformations, known limitations, and statistical properties — which is essential for responsible AI deployment.
Faster iteration: automating routine preprocessing tasks frees the ML engineer to focus on modeling decisions rather than data wrangling.

Transformation errors: automated transformations can be incorrect for the domain — an agent that imputes a missing 'blood pressure' value with the column mean may be wrong in a clinical context. Domain review of transformation decisions is essential.

Feature engineering creativity: agents generate features based on patterns in the data and heuristics, but domain expertise — knowing that the ratio of two specific columns is a meaningful predictor — is not reliably generated by general agents.
Computational cost: full data profiling on large datasets is expensive; profiling strategies must be adapted (sampling, incremental processing) for datasets beyond a few GB.

Over-engineering risk: agents may generate a large number of features that increase dimensionality and training cost without improving model performance; feature selection is a necessary downstream step.
Label noise handling: detecting and correcting label noise (incorrect labels in the training set) requires domain knowledge and human judgment that preprocessing agents alone cannot provide.
The preprocessing agent pipeline proceeds in four phases:

Data profiling: the orchestrator samples the dataset and computes per-column statistics (dtype, null rate, unique value count, distribution shape, outlier fraction, correlation with label) to build a data profile that guides transformation decisions.

Issue detection and cleaning plan: the agent analyzes the profile to detect quality issues (high null rates, inconsistent encoding, outliers, class imbalance, temporal leakage risk) and generates a cleaning plan that specifies the transformation strategy for each issue with rationale.

Parallel execution: specialist agents execute the cleaning plan — the cleaning agent handles missing values and duplicates, the feature engineering agent generates and encodes features, running in parallel where operations are independent.

Validation and documentation: the validation agent checks the processed dataset against schema contracts and statistical expectations (label distribution preserved, no null values remain in required columns, feature distributions within expected bounds). The quality reporter generates the dataset version record and data card.
Statistical tests; heuristic rules for each data type
Imputation strategy selection based on task type and null pattern
Domain-appropriate features based on data type and task
Schema contract checks; statistical distribution tests
Structured documentation template with computed statistics
Data preprocessing agents are most valuable when:
The team handles multiple datasets with varied structures and quality issues; the agent's profiling and adaptive transformation logic is more efficient than writing custom scripts per dataset.
Reproducibility and documentation are important — regulated domains (healthcare, finance) require documented data lineage; the agent produces this as a natural output.
Feature engineering is largely standard for the domain (tabular ML, time series forecasting) — the agent can handle encoding, scaling, lag features, and rolling statistics systematically.
Data quality is a known issue — the agent's profiling systematically surfaces problems that might be missed in manual inspection.

Preprocessing agents add less value for: bespoke preprocessing that requires deep domain expertise for every transformation decision; datasets requiring manual label correction or expert annotation; very small datasets where preprocessing is trivial; or tasks where the raw data format is already well-suited to the model (e.g., raw text for a fine-tuned language model).

# Data preprocessing agent
import pandas as pd
import json
from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()

PROFILER_SYSTEM = """You are a data quality analyst. Given a dataset profile, identify:
1. Columns with quality issues (missing data, outliers, encoding problems, leakage risk)
2. Recommended preprocessing action for each issue with rationale
3. Suggested feature engineering steps for the task type

Be specific: name exact columns and recommend exact transformations.
Only recommend transformations that are standard and defensible — do not invent domain-specific rules."""

def profile_dataset(df: pd.DataFrame) -> dict:
    """Compute statistical profile of each column."""
    profile = {}
    for col in df.columns:
        series = df[col]
        profile[col] = {
            "dtype": str(series.dtype),
            "null_rate": float(series.isnull().mean()),
            "unique_count": int(series.nunique()),
            "sample_values": series.dropna().head(5).tolist(),
        }
        if pd.api.types.is_numeric_dtype(series):
            profile[col].update({
                "mean": float(series.mean()),
                "std": float(series.std()),
                "min": float(series.min()),
                "max": float(series.max()),
                "skewness": float(series.skew()),
            })
    return profile

def generate_cleaning_plan(profile: dict, task_spec: dict) -> dict:
    """Use LLM to generate a context-aware cleaning plan."""
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=PROFILER_SYSTEM,
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": (
                f"Task type: {task_spec['task_type']}\n"
                f"Target column: {task_spec['target']}\n"
                f"Dataset profile:\n{json.dumps(profile, indent=2)}"
            )
        }]
    )
    return json.loads(response.content[0].text)

def apply_cleaning(df: pd.DataFrame, plan: dict) -> tuple:
    """Apply the cleaning plan and log every transformation."""
    log = []
    df_clean = df.copy()
    
    for step in plan.get("cleaning_steps", []):
        col = step["column"]
        action = step["action"]
        
        if action == "impute_mean":
            fill_val = df_clean[col].mean()
            null_count = df_clean[col].isnull().sum()
            df_clean[col] = df_clean[col].fillna(fill_val)
            log.append({"col": col, "action": action, "fill_value": fill_val,
                        "rows_affected": int(null_count), "rationale": step["rationale"]})
        
        elif action == "drop_column":
            df_clean = df_clean.drop(columns=[col])
            log.append({"col": col, "action": action, "rationale": step["rationale"]})
        
        elif action == "encode_ordinal":
            mapping = {v: i for i, v in enumerate(sorted(df_clean[col].dropna().unique()))}
            df_clean[col] = df_clean[col].map(mapping)
            log.append({"col": col, "action": action, "mapping": mapping,
                        "rationale": step["rationale"]})
    
    return df_clean, log

def preprocess(df: pd.DataFrame, task_spec: dict) -> dict:
    profile = profile_dataset(df)
    plan = generate_cleaning_plan(profile, task_spec)
    df_clean, log = apply_cleaning(df, plan)
    
    return {
        "dataset": df_clean,
        "transformation_log": log,
        "profile": profile,
        "plan": plan
    }

A customer churn prediction dataset has 40 columns with varying null rates. The profiler detects: 'account_age' has 12% nulls (impute with median), 'last_login' has 3% nulls (forward-fill within customer ID), 'plan_type' is a high-cardinality categorical with 150 values (embed or target-encode), and 'daily_usage_mb' is right-skewed with outliers (log-transform). The agent generates the cleaning plan, executes it, and logs each decision.

A sales forecasting dataset has timestamp gaps, negative values in 'units_sold' (likely data errors), and no temporal features. The cleaning agent forward-fills gaps, caps negative values at zero with a log entry, and the feature engineering agent generates: day-of-week, month, lag-7, lag-14, and rolling-28-day mean features — standard time series features appropriate for the task.

A text classification dataset has inconsistent encoding (mixed UTF-8 and Latin-1), HTML tags in some entries, and duplicates. The cleaning agent detects and normalizes encoding, strips HTML, and removes exact duplicates. A near-duplicate check using MinHash LSH is flagged as a recommendation for human review before automatic deduplication.

After preprocessing, the quality reporter generates a data card: source, collection dates, transformations applied (with counts of affected rows), remaining known issues, statistical summary of key features, and a flag that the dataset is appropriate for the stated task with one caveat (class imbalance: 92% negative, 8% positive) — documented for model training teams.

An e-commerce company's data science team spent an average of three days on data preprocessing for each new experiment — profiling columns, deciding on imputation strategies, engineering features, and writing validation checks. After deploying a preprocessing agent, the average preprocessing time dropped to four hours for familiar dataset structures and eight hours for novel datasets requiring domain review of agent recommendations.

The agent profiled a 2.1M-row transaction dataset, detected 23 quality issues across 67 columns (high null rates in 8 columns, encoding inconsistency in 3 categorical columns, temporal leakage from 2 date columns relative to the prediction target), generated a cleaning plan with rationale for each decision, applied transformations in 12 minutes (parallel across column groups), and produced a data card documenting the full transformation history. The data science team reviewed the plan before execution and approved it with two modifications — changing the imputation strategy for one column from mean to forward-fill based on domain knowledge. The resulting pipeline was reusable: when the next month's dataset arrived with the same schema, preprocessing was fully automated.

The most costly preprocessing error is including features that contain information about the future target — a preprocessing agent must explicitly check for temporal ordering and leakage from derived columns. Always flag potential leakage columns for human review.
Applying transformations without logging the decision — including which rows were affected, the original distribution, and the strategy rationale — makes the pipeline unreproducible and unauditable.

Preprocessing steps that compute statistics (mean for imputation, scaling parameters, encoding mappings) must be fit on the training split only, then applied to validation and test sets. Fitting on the full dataset leaks test-set statistics into training.

Applying transformations without validating the output schema and statistical properties allows errors to propagate silently into training. Define and check schema contracts for the processed dataset.
Near-duplicate detection for text or image data requires human judgment before removal — automatically deduplicating may remove legitimately different examples. Flag for review; do not auto-delete.
Profile the dataset before writing any transformation code. The profile reveals the actual problems; assumptions about data quality are frequently wrong.
Log every transformation with: column name, action taken, original value or distribution, new value or distribution, rows affected, and decision rationale. This log is the reproducibility record.
Enforce train/validation/test split before any statistics-based transformation. Compute imputation values, scaling parameters, and encoding mappings from the training split only.
Define a schema contract for the processed dataset and validate against it as the final pipeline step. Failed validation should halt the pipeline and surface an informative error.
Flag suspicious transformations for human review rather than applying them automatically: near-duplicate detection, column drops due to high null rates, and columns detected as potential leakage.
Version the processed dataset with a content hash and store the transformation log alongside it. Any training run should be reproducible from the stored log and the raw data hash.

Data profiling and cleaning on large datasets (hundreds of millions of rows) requires strategies beyond loading the full dataset into pandas memory. For profiling: compute statistics on a stratified sample (10,000-100,000 rows) for distribution estimates, and run schema and null checks on the full dataset using chunked processing or SQL queries against the source database. For transformation: execute cleaning operations in parallel across column groups where operations are independent; use Spark, Dask, or DuckDB for datasets that do not fit in memory.

LLM calls in the preprocessing pipeline are fast relative to data processing: the agent call for cleaning plan generation (a one-time call per dataset) adds seconds, not hours. The compute cost of the pipeline is dominated by data processing, not LLM inference.
ydata-profiling produces detailed HTML reports; Great Expectations adds schema contracts
Use for datasets above 1GB; Polars offers pandas-like API with much better performance
Store engineered features with versioning for reuse across experiments
dbt is well-suited for SQL-based transformations on structured data
DVC integrates with Git for dataset version control alongside code
Full control; domain expertise applied directly
Time-intensive; ad-hoc; not documented; hard to reproduce
Black box; limited control; may not suit domain-specific requirements
Systematic; documented; adaptive to task context; reproducible
LLM transformation plan needs domain review; profiling cost on large datasets
Requires upfront schema definition; brittle to schema changes
Preprocessing pipelines often handle PII or confidential business data. Apply data masking or tokenization to sensitive columns before they enter the agent's context window or are logged.
Some transformations (column drops, encoding changes) are not reversible from the processed dataset alone. Always preserve the raw data and the transformation log — never overwrite raw data in place.
Preprocessing pipelines must produce identical outputs given identical inputs. Ensure that operations like random sampling (for oversampling) use seeded randomness and that the seed is logged.

When a new data batch arrives with schema changes (new columns, renamed columns, changed dtypes), the preprocessing agent should detect the drift and alert rather than applying the previous pipeline silently.

A data preprocessing agent is best understood as an experienced data analyst who has preprocessed hundreds of datasets and knows the common patterns — not one who makes domain-specific judgments about clinical measurements or financial signals, but one who knows how to handle missing data, identify encoding inconsistencies, generate appropriate features for a tabular classification task, and document what they did. The agent is fast, systematic, and consistent — but it needs domain-expert review on any decision that requires knowledge beyond statistical patterns.

The transformation log is the agent's most important output. A cleaned dataset without a transformation history is opaque: you cannot reproduce it, audit it, or understand what was changed. A transformation history without a cleaned dataset is reconstructable. Design the agent to treat the log as the primary artifact and the cleaned dataset as the derived artifact.
Data preprocessing agents automate profiling, cleaning, feature engineering, and validation — but every transformation decision must be logged with rationale for reproducibility and auditability.
Train/validation/test split must happen before any statistics-based transformation; computing imputation values on the full dataset leaks test-set information into training.
The agent's cleaning plan requires domain review before execution for any transformations that involve domain-specific judgment (clinical values, financial constraints, label noise).
Output validation against a schema contract is a required pipeline step; silent errors in the processed dataset propagate into model training and are hard to diagnose.
Version the processed dataset with a content hash and store the transformation log alongside it — reproducibility requires both the data and the documented process that produced it.
